persons.arguments_against: The case notes 2 bios and 3 officials for the surname Hellings, so a same-surname relative (and 'cadet' is a generic title) is the worry. It fails because Hellings is a rare surname, the bio is the only one entangled with these stints, and both stints trace one continuous Ceylon career matching the bio by specific named posts. Stint 1 (1889-1910): cadet 1889 (bio 'cadet, Ceylon civ. ser., Dec., 1885'), Assistant Collector and Landing Surveyor in the Eastern Province 1890 (bio 'asst. collr., etc., customs, Trincomalee, Nov., 1888' -- Trincomalee is in the Eastern Province), Additional Commissioner of Requests and Police Magistrate (bio 'addl. pol. mag.... 1888' and the later Commissioner-of-Requests/judge posts at Colombo), then Assistant Government Agent 1906 and Government Agent 1907-10 (bio 'asst. gov't. agt., Matale, Nov., 1904; gov't. agt., Prov. of Sabangamuwa, Feb., 1906'). Stint 2 (1913-21): Collector / Government Agent of the Southern Province, matching the bio 'gov't. agt., S. Prov., May, 1912' exactly, and the bio editions run to 1921 alongside the stint. The tenure/position FAILs on stint 2 are mechanical (the bio's placed events are coded without province granularity); the printed Southern-Province agency is a direct match. No second R. B. Hellings competes for these posts.

evidence_cited.fact: R. B. Hellings | Assistant Collector and Landing Surveyor | Eastern Province
R. B. Hellings | Collector | Southern Province

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second R. B. Hellings in the Ceylon service holding the Eastern-Province customs collectorship and the Southern-Province agency -- which the rare surname and single competing bio make implausible.

notes: Robert Bailey Hellings, Ceylon civil servant. Rare surname; two stints are the early (1889-1910) and late (1913-21) segments of one continuous career. The Eastern-Province customs collectorship (Trincomalee) and the Southern-Province government agency match the bio by name. One stray 1890 'Master Attendant, Harbour Department' line co-occurs with the Eastern-Province collectorship within the 1889 stint and does not disturb the identification.
